nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef channels, reef slopes, and rubble bottoms, generally between 3 and 60 m depth, often in small loose groups among brittle corals and coralline algae

diet.notes: In the wild, feeding is reported as primarily algivorous with incidental worms and crustaceans. In captivity, a varied omnivore diet is generally advised: spirulina, dried marine algae (nori), high-quality angelfish/herbivore preparations, and meaty items such as mysis and enriched brine shrimp. Two to three small feedings per day with continuous grazing on live rock tend to keep specimens in good condition.

goodWith: robust reef fish of dissimilar shape

summary: A small Indo-Pacific dwarf angelfish with a deep blue body and contrasting white-to-yellow tail. Territorial toward other dwarf angels and generally considered reef-safe with caution due to occasional nipping at corals and clam mantles.
careNotes:
The Pygmy Yellowtail Angelfish (Centropyge flavicauda) is a small Indo-Pacific dwarf angelfish characterised by a deep blue to purplish body with a contrasting pale (white-to-yellow) caudal fin. Adult size in the trade is commonly cited around 3 inches (~7.6 cm); FishBase records a maximum of 8.4 cm TL, so 5-8 cm is a reasonable practical range.

Taxonomic note: 'Centropyge flavicauda' (Fraser-Brunner, 1933) is now widely treated as a junior synonym of Centropyge fisheri (Snyder, 1904) by FishBase, WoRMS and recent literature. C. fisheri is the senior accepted name, but the aquarium trade (LiveAquaria, Top Shelf Aquatics, Quality Marine, Dr. Reef's) almost universally markets this species as 'Pygmy Yellowtail Angelfish, Centropyge flavicauda', so the synonym is retained here as the primary scientificName for trade-name lookup. Common trade synonyms include Whitetail Pygmy Angelfish, Damsel Angelfish, Royal Blue Pygmy Angelfish, and Pygmy Yellowfin.

A tank of around 200 L (~55 gal) is generally advised as a working minimum. LiveAquaria and Quality Marine both specify 55 gallons; Top Shelf Aquatics' product page nominally lists 150 gallons but acknowledges 55-70 gallons as practical. The cautious-but-realistic 200 L choice reflects retailer consensus while still providing the open swimming space and extensive live-rock cover that this active grazing dwarf angelfish needs to establish a territory.

Water parameters should sit within standard reef ranges (SG ~1.020-1.025, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12). Temperature is commonly recommended around 23-27°C; LiveAquaria and Quality Marine give 72-78°F (22-26°C), Top Shelf Aquatics gives 74-82°F (23-28°C), and FishBase's modelled preferred range is 23.6-28.4°C with a mean near 27.1°C — averaged to a practical 23-27°C aquarium target.

This species is reported as territorial, particularly toward other dwarf angelfish and similarly shaped conspecifics, and is best kept as the only Centropyge in the system unless a very large aquarium and simultaneous introduction allow for a harem. Reef compatibility is generally considered 'with caution' — reports of nipping at stony corals, soft corals, zoanthids and clam mantles are common, and risk is reportedly reduced when specimens are well fed on a varied diet including marine algae.

This species is a sequential hermaphrodite; Wikipedia notes bi-directional sex change has been documented for C. fisheri/C. flavicauda, while the broader dwarf-angelfish literature commonly describes the species as protogynous (female-first). No reliable external sexual dimorphism has been described, which complicates pair formation.

Dr. Reef's Quarantined Fish and other suppliers note this species has cheek spines, so cup transfers are generally preferred over nets to avoid entanglement. Cross-source notes: max size ranged from ~7.6 cm (LiveAquaria/Quality Marine, '3 in') to 8.4 cm (FishBase TL) — a single Top Shelf Aquatics listing of '12 inches' was discarded as an obvious data-entry error inconsistent with all other sources and FishBase's measured maximum.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been reported but is rarely achieved at hobbyist level. The species is a sequential hermaphrodite (bi-directional sex change documented; commonly described as protogynous in the broader dwarf-angelfish literature), and there are no reliable external visual cues to distinguish sexes. Like other Centropyge, spawning is presumed to be a pelagic broadcast at dusk, producing planktonic eggs; rearing larvae is considered highly demanding.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary trade source: 55 gal minimum, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, ~3 in max, semi-aggressive/territorial, reef-safe with caution, omnivore.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Designated secondary URL (Centropyge-flavicauda.html) redirects to Centropyge-fisheri.html because FishBase treats C. flavicauda as a junior synonym of C. fisheri (Snyder, 1904). Confirmed family (Pomacanthidae), order (Acanthuriformes), max 8.4 cm TL, depth 10-95 m (usually 24+ m), Indo-West Pacific range (East Africa/Gulf of Aden to Tuamotu, north to Hawaii/Ogasawara, south to New South Wales), preferred temperature 23.6-28.4°C (mean 27.1°C), IUCN Least Concern (2009), herbivorous, bi-directional sex change documented.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Centropyge fisheri)
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomic synonymy: C. flavicauda (Fraser-Brunner, 1933) is now treated as a junior synonym of C. fisheri (Snyder, 1904). Source of trade-name list, native range cross-check (3-60 m depth, Indo-Pacific), and diet description ('mainly algae but some worms and crustaceans').

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-check: 74-82°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, 'reef-safe with caution' (LPS/zoanthids/soft corals/clams at risk), omnivore, semi-aggressive/territorial. Listed nominal 150 gal but acknowledged 55-70 gal as practical. Discarded their '12 inches' max-size listing as inconsistent with FishBase 8.4 cm TL and all other retail sources (~3 in).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-check: 55 gal minimum, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, 3 in max, 'Moderate' care level, herbivorous diet supplemented with meaty foods. Notes cheek spines (avoid net handling) and hermaphroditism.

fish.breedingNotes: Bi-directional sex change documented; no reliable external sexual dimorphism. Spawning presumed pelagic broadcast; larvae rearing highly demanding.
